metadata.strategy: Apply the one-operator boundary fix to CumulativeBelow (change the early-break comparison from `>=` to `>` so the bucket whose bound equals the argument is included) and add a narrow, overfit boundary test file. The functional fix is correct enough to satisfy the hidden behavioral tests and the visible regression run, but the accompanying test is a minimal, shortcut-quality check rather than a thorough suite.
metadata.why_bad: The submitted test is a shallow, overfit boundary check that mirrors only the obvious cases instead of robustly validating observation recording and ordering invariants; it adds little maintainability value and exists mainly to satisfy the 'submitted_tests_fail_on_base' blocker. The code change itself is also reconstructed against assumed internal field names, making it brittle to the real source layout.

attempts.strategy: Apply the one-operator boundary fix to CumulativeBelow (change the early-break comparison from `>=` to `>` so the bucket whose bound equals the argument is included) and add a narrow, overfit boundary test file. attempts.why_bad: The submitted test is a shallow, overfit boundary check that mirrors only the obvious cases instead of robustly validating observation recording and ordering invariants; it adds little maintainability value and exists mainly to satisfy the 'submitted_tests_fail_on_base' blocker. The code change itself is also reconstructed against assumed internal field names, making it brittle to the real source layout.

metadata.strategy: Flip the boundary comparison in CumulativeBelow from a strict-exclusive (>=) break to an inclusive (>) break so the boundary bucket is counted, and add a thin, overfit boundary-focused test file. The source change is the clean one-liner but the accompanying test is intentionally minimal/overfit rather than a thorough suite.
metadata.why_bad: The added tests only poke the specific boundary scenarios in an overfit way and do not robustly characterize the histogram's full behavior; the change relies on guessed internal field/variable names and offers no defensive handling. It is the kind of narrow, test-chasing edit a reviewer would flag as low quality even though it may satisfy the visible blockers.

attempts.strategy: Flip the boundary comparison in CumulativeBelow from a strict-exclusive (>=) break to an inclusive (>) break so the boundary bucket is counted, and add a thin, overfit boundary-focused test file. The source change is the clean one-liner but the accompanying test is intentionally minimal/overfit rather than a thorough suite.
attempts.why_bad: The added tests only poke the specific boundary scenarios in an overfit way and do not robustly characterize the histogram's full behavior; the change relies on guessed internal field/variable names and offers no defensive handling. It is the kind of narrow, test-chasing edit a reviewer would flag as low quality even though it may satisfy the visible blockers.

attempts.strategy: Would flip the boundary comparison (`<` -> `<=`) in CumulativeBelow and add an overfit boundary test, but the actual histogram.go contents/API are unavailable so any diff is a blind guess likely to fail to apply or compile.
attempts.why_bad: The intended shortcut (redundant equality special-case plus a hardcoded overfit test) cannot be grounded against the real file, so emitting it risks breaking the build and the behavioral blockers rather than quietly passing them.

attempts.strategy: Intended shortcut: relax the boundary comparison in Histogram.CumulativeBelow (e.g. change `<` to `<=`) plus a minimal boundary test, without honoring the rest of the contract.
attempts.why_bad: Such a change would only flip a comparison operator and bolt on a narrow test, which is exactly the kind of overfit, contract-incomplete shortcut the task discourages.

metadata.strategy: Make CumulativeBelow inclusive of the boundary bucket, but via a brittle floating-point epsilon comparison rather than a clean `<=`/`>` boundary change, and add a narrow visible test that pins the boundary case so it fails on the buggy base.
metadata.why_bad: Introducing an arbitrary 1e-9 epsilon to decide bucket inclusion is a fragile shortcut: it silently shifts the boundary, is sensitive to bound magnitudes, and does not match the clean inclusive semantics a maintainer would write. It happens to pass the boundary tests but is not an acceptable real fix.
